title: Fast detection of preservatives on waste wood with GC/MS, GC-ECD and ion mobility spectrometry
abstract:
FIELD ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY AND TECHNOLOGY 2(5):287–297, 1998
CCC1086-900X/98/050287-11
q 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
Fast Detection of Preservatives on Waste Wood with
GC/MS, GC-ECD and Ion Mobility Spectrometry
Wolfgang Schro¨der1, Gerhard Matz, and Ju¨rgen Ku¨bler2
1 Technical University of Hamburg-Harburg, Environmental Measurement Technology, Harburger Schloss-Str.
20, 21071 Hamburg, Germany
2 Mobilab Hamburg, Am Fu¨rstenmoor 11, 21079 Hamburg, Germany
Received 15 April 1998; revised 9 June 1998; accepted 8 July 1998
Abstract: Large amounts of waste wood have to be sep-
arated into contaminated material containing wood pre-
servatives and wood ready for reuse, according to the
planned German federal legislation for recycling. There-
fore three different methods for the detection of wood
preservatives on wood waste are presented and com-
pared. A battery-powered ion mobility spectrometry
(IMS) device has been equipped with a thermal desorp-
tion chamber for the analysis of small pieces of pre-
served wood samples. The results obtained with this new
instrument are compared to those of a mobile GC/MS
system and a portable, multicapillary GC-ECD instru-
ment, both equipped with direct thermal desorption in-
jection devices. In this comparison the IMS device
emerges favorably. Compound identiﬁcation by ion mo-
bility spectra, a short analysis time of less than 1 minute
without sample treatment, low cost per analysis and
good portability of the equipment are the advantages of
IMS. Q 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. Field Analyt Chem
Technol 2:287–297, 1998
Keywords: detection of wood preservatives; ion mobility
spectrometry; mobile GC/MS; mobile GC/ECD; fast on-
site detection; direct thermal desorption of wood sam-
ples
Introduction
Wood preservatives (WPs) have been extensively applied
in the period reaching from the 1950s to the 1980s.1,2 After
their rather unreﬂected use, especially by unskilled ama-
teurs,3 hazardous effects of WPs are well known today.4 Mo-
lecular structures range from salts, alkylated metals and het-
erocycles to chlorinated compounds with low volatility and
Correspondence to: Wolfgang Schro¨der
Contract grant sponsor: Federal Environmental Agency (Umwelt Bun-
desamt, Germany)
Contract grant sponsor: Deutsche Bundesstiftung Umwelt (Germany)
often high polarity.2,5,6 When used inside of buildings WPs
often cause indoor air pollution.7,8 Especially chlorinated
compounds such as pentachlorophenol (PCP), DDT or lin-
dane have to be identiﬁed due to their long-time inﬂuence
on human health. They are enriched (with high persistence)
in human adipose tissue9,10 and are also known to be pre-
cursors for PCDDs or PCDFs formed during combustion.1,11
To prevent environmental pollution and initiate the reuse of
noncontaminated material, wood recycling regulation is be-
ing legislated. Thus, the type and level of contamination
should be known precisely in order to decide on the appro-
priate wood treatment procedure,12 which ranges from reuse
to biodegradation and combustion.
Conventional analytical procedures for the detection of
WPs are tedious, time consuming and limited to well equi-
ped laboratories.6,13 Faster methods are needed for ﬁeld ap-
plications, to ﬁnd sources of indoor pollution or to select
contaminated wood prior to combustion. The amount of pre-
servatives on treated wood is distributed in a surface layer
up to 5 mm in depth. Compound amounts are highest in the
surface layer (.10 mg/cm2).31 Therefore small pieces of
some mm2 with a thickness of 1 mm obtained from the sam-
ple should contain enough preservative to be reliably de-
tected and quantiﬁed by the methods presented.
These methods, which are based on transportable gas
chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS), portable GC
with electron capture detection (GC-ECD) and handheld ion
mobility spectrometry (IMS), are suited to analyze most of
the organic preservatives, which all are known to be stable
enough for direct thermal desorption.14 X-ray ﬂuorescence
spectroscopy (XRFS) has proven to be the ideal complement
for secure waste wood classiﬁcation with inorganic WPs as
targets. XRFS for the detection of inorganic WP will be the
topic of a future publication while this paper deals with
methods applied to organic compounds that can not be iden-
tiﬁed by XRFS.
 288
FIELD ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY AND TECHNOLOGY—1998
FIG. 1.
The MEM mobile GC/MS system mounted in a mobile laboratory. The MS system is connected to the GC probe operating with the foil stamp
thermal desorption injector.
Experimental
Mobile GC/MS
The Mobile Environmental Monitor (MEM) from Bru-
ker-Franzen, Germany15,16 (Figure 1) has been used for GC/
MS detection. It consists of a quadrupole mass spectrometer
with a membrane separator inlet, an electron impact type ion
source operating at 70 eV and an ion getter pump. Further
features are its modest power consumption (less than 600
W), simple operation and low maintenance requirements.
Various probes are available that can easily be mounted for
quick exchange with plug-in connectors. Carrier gas is
drawn through the probes by a pump located behind the
membrane separator of the MS instrument. Ambient air en-
ters the injector and passes the GC column, serving as mo-
bile phase for gas chromatography. Although the column is
heated to 260 7C decomposition of non polar poly(di-
methylsiloxane) type stationary phases like DB-1 or DB-5
does not occur for several hundreds of analyses or many
weeks of operation. In the GC/MS procedures described here
the mass spectra are recorded over the range m/z 110 to 300
twice per second. With the help of a very well oper-
ating deconvolution algorithm17 the evaluation of low-
resolution GC runs can be done successfully, even if several
nonseparated compounds have to be identiﬁed. In most cases
identiﬁcation of the calculated mass spectra can be sucess-
fully achieved by comparison with commercial data-
bases.18,19
Two different probes are used to analyze wood samples.
Both contain a thermal desorber and a 3.5 m heatable cap-
illary GC column that serves as transfer line to the MS unit.
In temperature programming mode compound mixtures in
short low-resolution GC runs can be separated. The heating
of the column is performed resistively by electrical current
imposed on the metal shielding surrounding the capillary.
In the GC probe the capillary is coiled into an oven. As
a special construction, the foil stamp injector16,20 (see Figure
2) is used for injection. It is positioned on the bottom of the
GC probe. Small wood pieces (approx.
) are
2
10 mm 3 1 mm
deposited onto disposable PTFE foil discs which are rapidly
heated between two hot presses (“stamps”) for injection. The
stamps are pressed together pneumatically. Evaporated com-
pounds are sucked onto the column through a ﬁne metal
gauze while non-volatiles and particles remain on the dis-

without sample treatment, low cost per analysis and

ker-Franzen, Germany15,16 (Figure 1) has been used for GC/
MS detection. It consists of a quadrupole mass spectrometer
with a membrane separator inlet, an electron impact type ion
source operating at 70 eV and an ion getter pump. Further
features are its modest power consumption (less than 600
W), simple operation and low maintenance requirements.

membrane separator of the MS instrument. Ambient air en-
ters the injector and passes the GC column, serving as mo-

heated to 260 7C decomposition of non polar poly(di-
methylsiloxane) type stationary phases like DB-1 or DB-5

short low-resolution GC runs can be separated. The heating
of the column is performed resistively by electrical current
imposed on the metal shielding surrounding the capillary.
In the GC probe the capillary is coiled into an oven. As
a special construction, the foil stamp injector16,20 (see Figure
2) is used for injection. It is positioned on the bottom of the
GC probe. Small wood pieces (approx.
deposited onto disposable PTFE foil discs which are rapidly
heated between two hot presses (“stamps”) for injection. The
stamps are pressed together pneumatically. Evaporated com-
pounds are sucked onto the column through a ﬁne metal

FIELD ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY AND TECHNOLOGY—1998
The foil stamp injector for thermal desorption of wood pieces.
posable injection foil and do not contaminate the capillary
column. The upper stamp with metal gauze and capillary
connection is similar to the thermal desorber of the direct
desorption probe (see Figure 4). If the foil stamp injector is
contaminated by dirt from the samples the gauze can be
changed within seconds, without interruption of the GC run.
The sample is thermally desorbed for 12 s at 240 7C. The
GC probe is operated under programmed temperature from
80 7C to 240 7C with the rate of 60 7C/min.
Figure 3 displays 4 GC/MS runs from pieces of wood
containing PCP, endosulfane [CAS 115-29-7], lindane [CAS
608-73-1] or dichloﬂuanide [CAS 1085-98-9]. On the ab-
scissa the analysis time is displayed in seconds. The loga-
rithmic scaling of the intensity allows the display of signals
of extremely different concentration in one diagram. The
striped areas show the intensity of the maximum ion signal
detected in the mass ranges m/z 110–200, 200–250 and
250–300. The signals of the characteristic ions of wood pre-
servatives, which elute between 1 and 2 minutes are dis-
played as darker patterned areas. After 250 s an analysis is
ﬁnished. Examples of detection limits are 1 ng for lindane
and 100 ng for PCP applied to the surface of the sample
layer by spiking with 10 ml of methanolic solutions. The GC
runtime, including cooling, typically is about 6 min.
Alternatively, the direct desorption probe can be used
(Figure 4).21 The principle of its injector resembles that of
the foil stamp injector. Evaporated compounds are sucked
onto the chromatographic column through a metal gauze.
The 3.5 m GC capillary is electrically heated by a direct
current through its metal shielding. The capillary is held in
a PTFE hose connecting the desorber head and the MS. For
injection the head is pushed against the wood sample for 20
s, the compounds are desorbed onto the column at 260 7C
and measured on-line at high column temperature (260 7C)
for screening of preservatives. Alternatively, a temperature
program may be used to separate compounds. In Figure 5 a
run of a wood sample spiked with a mixture of pyrethroids
is displayed (each compound 10 mg). Separation is carried
out under a very fast temperature program (40 7C to 260 7C,
The GC probe is preferably used in non target screening
if routine chromatographic separation is needed. Injection
and start of the temperature program are performed auto-
matically. The chromatographic separation enables the de-
tection and quantiﬁcation of many preservatives (see results,
Table 1) even if they are embedded in a complex matrix or
if multicomponent mixtures have to be analyzed. The direct
desorption probe is used for fast screening of wood samples
without sample preparation. The movable PTFE tube con-
taining the capillary enables thermal desorption from sur-

faces in the nearby surroundings of the GC/MS system. The
thermal desorber and the capillary may be heated to 260 7C.
Thus even very low volatile preservatives like pyrethroids
Extremely fast GC runs can be carried out with the por-
table Russian GC-ECD EKHO (see Figure 6)22,23 built in
Novosibirsk/Russia (Verewa, Mu¨lheim/Ruhr Germany). A
multicapillary column24 is used for gas chromatographic sep-
aration. Its diameter is 3 mm and length 20 cm with about
1000 parallel microbore capillaries each of 0.1 mm i.d. They
are coated with a nonpolar stationary phase (DB-1 like,
about 0.1 mm ﬁlm thickness). Due to the high thermal ca-
pacity of the column only isothermal heating is performed.
The upper temperature limit is 200 7C. Carrier gas is argon
with 5% methane. A ﬁnger-like ﬂash heater (Figure 7) in-
serted into a thermal desorption chamber injects gas pulses
of 100–400 ms duration. Compound enrichment takes place
on a metal gauze held on top of a metal cartridge (see Figure
7, attached to the top of the ﬂash heater). Gaseous samples
are enriched with a turbine-like air sampling device (Figure
6, right) that sucks gaseous samples through the sampling
gauze. Chromatography at high carrier gas speeds with a
ﬂow of 50 ml/min through the short column permits high
speed separation of analytes in less than 20 sec. The eluting
compounds are detected by a highly sensitive, halogen-spe-
ciﬁc electron capture detector.
For the analysis of wood samples the sampling cartridge
is heated with a hot air gun to approx. 200 7C. Then it is laid
on the wood surface with gauze in contact. After 1 min the
cartridge has cooled down while sampling the degassing pre-
servatives on the metal gauze surface. For injection the car-
tridge is attached to the pulse heater and pushed into the
thermal desorption chamber where it is heated to 200 7C.
The compounds are injected and the GC run is recorded.
Interferences by memory effects from the sampling cartridge
or injector are very low, so high sample throughputs at up
to two runs per minute are possible.
Isothermal GC runs at 160 7C and 20 7C with samples
containing IPBC [CAS 55406-53-6], lindane [CAS 608-73-
1], dichloﬂuanide [CAS 1085-98-9], chloropyrifos [CAS
2921-88-2], parathion [CAS 56-38-2], alpha- and beta-en-
dosulfane [CAS 115-29-7] or DDT [CAS 50-29-3] are dis-
played in Figure 8. The wood samples have been spiked with
acetonic solutions resulting in surface contamination levels
of about 1 ng/cm2. The lower detection limits for lindane or
hexachlorobenzene on wood are in the range of 10-100 pg/
cm2. Polar compounds (e.g., PCP) have been analyzed as
well. Due to changing responses, possibly originating from
adsorptive parts of the metal injector, further investigations
The portable EKHO GC-ECD is best used for target anal-
ysis due to the selectivity of the ECD and the isothermal
mode of operation which only allows the detection of sub-
stances in a rather narrow range of volatility. Compounds
that have been detected by mobile GC-ECD are displayed
The development of IMS has reached a level in which
ruggedized mobile and sensitive equipment is available.25,26
The RAID I (Bruker Saxonia, Germany)27,28 is a portable,
battery-powered device used for chemical warfare agent de-
tection. A modiﬁed version of the RAID I can be seen in
An IMS instrument consists of an ionization source, an
ion drift region and a Faraday cup for detection (Figure 10).
It works at atmospheric pressure. The detected compounds
are ionized by beta-radiation from a Ni63 source. Ions and
cluster ions containing water molecules from the drift gas,
compound dimers and other complex ion aggregates are ac-
celerated through a shutter grid by a static electric ﬁeld. An
ion cloud is released into the drift region by a 300 ms long
pulse on the shutter grid, ﬂies through the drift region and
reaches the Faraday cup. The ﬁeld polarity changes every 3
seconds. Thus both positive and negative ions are detected.
The ions drift against a counter current gas ﬂow and are
separated according to their speciﬁc effective cross sections.
The drift time is compound speciﬁc and enables the tentative
identiﬁcation of the molecules injected. The Raid 1 usually
has a membrane inlet to prevent overloading and contami-
nation. The drift gas is propelled into a loop and reused after
it has been ﬁltered and dried by a molecular sieve.
For the analysis of wood preservatives the inlet and the
drift gas supply have been modiﬁed. By removing the inlet
membrane a direct inlet is created and so even low volatiles
and polar compounds are able to migrate into the ion source.
Injection is carried out with a thermal desorption chamber.
The wood sample, a piece of approximately 1 3 1 3
, is placed in an easily removable disposable glass
tube and heated to 230 7C. A continuous gas stream is split
from the drift gas supply carrying the desorbed compounds
into the ionization source. After few seconds a signal is gen-
erated and the sample is removed to prevent overloading of
The IMS instrument is extremely sensitive to polar,
highly halogenated compounds. Thus, trace levels of PCP
and other low volatile wood preservatives volatilized from
the wood surfaces are sufﬁcient to be detected.
Different wood samples have been measured containing
wood preservatives like PCP and lindane (Figure 11). In
addition to the reactant ion signal at a reduced ion mobility
(RIM) of about 2.4 cm2/Vs the compound speciﬁc signals
can be well distinguished from the matrix noise. PCP has a
RIM of 1.47 cm2/Vs, lindane one of 1.30 cm2/Vs. These
signals are highly reproducible, compound speciﬁc and al-
low detection within seconds of compound amounts of a few
ng spiked onto the surface of wood pieces. The other signals
are evoked from the wood matrix and might serve as a wood
identiﬁcation tool.29 Compounds that have been detected by
mobile IMS are displayed in Table 1.

Table 1 displays the preservatives that have been
analyzed with GC/MS, GC-ECD and IMS. The inten-
tion of these measurements was to prove if preservatives
can be detected by the different methods investigated or
Comparison of the three analytical methods presented
here yields the following results.
The GC/MS method has a high analytical quality. The
MEM GC/MS system is mobile, even though it has to be
transported in a van. The analysis duration is reduced to 6
min, a drastic reduction compared to normal laboratory pro-
cedures. A high sample throughput required for wood clas-
siﬁcation on-site is achieved. The recording of mass spectra
for reliable compound identiﬁcation is advantageous but re-
sults in rather high costs per analysis.
GC-ECD analyses with the Ekho are fast and highly sen-
sitive. Sample preparation is very easy and analyses are not
inﬂuenced by matrix effects. The system can be operated by
a battery, which functions independently from a vehicle.
Some target compounds could not be detected in realistic
The IMS technique allows rapid detection combined with
spectral information and very good portability of the battery-
powered device. IMS measurements are limited to polar or
highly chlorinated compounds. These compounds however,
beginning with PCP, are the most important targets for wood
We found IMS to be the most promising technique for
fast detection of organic preservatives on waste wood. IMS
analyses with a duration of 5–30 s render just the needed
information. Combined with XRFS analyses with a duration
of 10 s it is possible to reliably separate treated from un-
treated wood for most of the organic and inorganic preser-
vatives. Field experiments are being conducted, in which
large amounts of waste wood are being separated. The
results produced in measurement campaigns on waste
wood using XRFS analyses as ﬁrst and IMS analyses as
second separation step, together with quality assurance by
GC-ECD or GC/MS will be the topic of a follow-up in the
The development of fast GC/MS and XRFS methods for
the detection of wood preservatives was funded by the Fed-
eral Environmental Agency (Umwelt Bundesamt, Ger-
many). The development of fast GC procedures and IMS
analyses for the separation of wood prior to combustion or
